**William Conrad** (born **John William Cann Jr.**, September 27, 1920 – February 11, 1994) was an American actor, producer, and director whose entertainment career spanned five decades in radio, film, and television.

A radio writer and actor, he moved to Hollywood after serving in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as a [fighter pilot](/wiki/Fighter_pilot "Fighter pilot") and played a series of character roles in films, beginning with the [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir") *[The Killers](/wiki/The_Killers_(1946_film) "The Killers (1946 film)")* (1946). He originated the role of [Marshal Matt Dillon](/wiki/Marshal_Matt_Dillon "Marshal Matt Dillon") for the radio series *[Gunsmoke](/wiki/Gunsmoke "Gunsmoke")* (1952–1961) and narrated the television adventures of *[Rocky and Bullwinkle](/wiki/Rocky_and_Bullwinkle "Rocky and Bullwinkle")* (1959–1964), *[Dudley Do-Right of the Mounties](/wiki/Dudley_Do-Right "Dudley Do-Right")* (1959–1964), *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1963_TV_series) "The Fugitive (1963 TV series)")* (1963–1967), and *[Hoppity Hooper](/wiki/Hoppity_Hooper "Hoppity Hooper")* (1964–1967).

Finding fewer onscreen roles in the 1950s, he changed from actor to producer-director with television work, narration, and a series of [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") films in the 1960s. Conrad found stardom as a detective in the TV series *[Cannon](/wiki/Cannon_(TV_series) "Cannon (TV series)")* (1971–1976) and *[Nero Wolfe](/wiki/Nero_Wolfe_(1981_TV_series) "Nero Wolfe (1981 TV series)")* (1981) and as district attorney Jason Lochinvar "J. L., Fatman" McCabe in the legal drama *[Jake and the Fatman](/wiki/Jake_and_the_Fatman "Jake and the Fatman")* (1987–1992).

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

William Conrad (also known as John William Conrad) was born John William Cann Jr., on September 27, 1920, in Louisville, Kentucky.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-1930_Census-2) His parents, John William Cann and Ida Mae Upchurch Cann, owned a movie theatre,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[2]](#cite_note-1930_Census-2) and Conrad grew up watching movies. The family moved to Southern California where as William Cann he attended [Excelsior Union High School](/wiki/Excelsior_Union_High_School "Excelsior Union High School") in [Norwalk](/wiki/Norwalk,_California "Norwalk, California"). He majored in drama and literature at [Fullerton College](/wiki/Fullerton_College "Fullerton College"), in Orange County, California, and began his career as an announcer, writer, and director for Los Angeles radio station [KMPC](/wiki/KSPN_(AM) "KSPN (AM)").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Conrad served as a [fighter pilot](/wiki/Fighter_pilot "Fighter pilot") in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). On the day he was commissioned in 1943 at [Luke Field](/wiki/Luke_Air_Force_Base "Luke Air Force Base"), he married June Nelson (1920–1977) of Los Angeles.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He left the [United States Army Air Forces](/wiki/United_States_Army_Air_Forces "United States Army Air Forces") with the rank of captain and as a producer-director of the [Armed Forces Radio Service](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Radio_Service "Armed Forces Radio Service").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Radio")]

Conrad estimated that he played more than 7,500 roles during his radio career.[[7]](#cite_note-William_Conrad-7) At [KMPC](/wiki/KSPN_(AM) "KSPN (AM)"), the 22-year-old Conrad produced and acted in *[The Hermit's Cave](/wiki/The_Hermit%27s_Cave "The Hermit's Cave")* (c. 1940–1944), the Los Angeles incarnation of a popular syndicated horror anthology series created at [WJR](/wiki/WJR "WJR") Detroit.[[8]](#cite_note-Dunning-8)

Conrad was among the supporting cast for the espionage drama *[The Man Called X](/wiki/The_Man_Called_X "The Man Called X")* (1944–1948);[[9]](#cite_note-9) the syndicated dramatic anthology *[Favorite Story](/wiki/Favorite_Story "Favorite Story")* (1946–1949);[[10]](#cite_note-10) the adventure dramas *[The Count of Monte Cristo](/wiki/The_Count_of_Monte_Cristo_(radio_program) "The Count of Monte Cristo (radio program)")* (Mutual 1947–1948),[[11]](#cite_note-11) *[The Voyage of the Scarlet Queen](/wiki/Voyage_of_the_Scarlet_Queen "Voyage of the Scarlet Queen")* (Mutual 1947–1948),[[12]](#cite_note-12) *[The Green Lama](/wiki/The_Green_Lama_(radio_series) "The Green Lama (radio series)")* (CBS 1949),[[13]](#cite_note-13) and *[Night Beat](/wiki/Night_Beat_(radio_program) "Night Beat (radio program)")* (NBC 1950–1952);[[14]](#cite_note-14) *Romance* (1950);[[15]](#cite_note-15) *[Hollywood Star Playhouse](/wiki/Hollywood_Star_Playhouse "Hollywood Star Playhouse")* (1950–1953);[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Errol Flynn](/wiki/Errol_Flynn "Errol Flynn")'s *[The Modern Adventures of Casanova](/wiki/The_Modern_Adventures_of_Casanova "The Modern Adventures of Casanova")* (Mutual 1952);[[17]](#cite_note-17) and Cathy and [Elliott Lewis](/wiki/Elliott_Lewis_(actor) "Elliott Lewis (actor)")'s *[On Stage](/wiki/On_Stage_(radio_show) "On Stage (radio show)")* (CBS 1953–1954).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Conrad was the voice of *[Escape](/wiki/Escape_(radio_program) "Escape (radio program)")* (1947–1954), a high-adventure radio series.[[19]](#cite_note-19) He played Warchek, a menacing policeman, in *[Johnny Modero: Pier 23](/wiki/Johnny_Modero,_Pier_23 "Johnny Modero, Pier 23")* (Mutual 1947),[[20]](#cite_note-20) a detective series starring [Jack Webb](/wiki/Jack_Webb "Jack Webb"), and was in the cast of Webb's crime dramas *[Dragnet](/wiki/Dragnet_(franchise) "Dragnet (franchise)")* (NBC, 1947–1951) and *[Pete Kelly's Blues](/wiki/Pete_Kelly%27s_Blues_(radio_series) "Pete Kelly's Blues (radio series)")* (NBC, 1951).[[21]](#cite_note-21) He played newspaper editor Walter Burns opposite [Dick Powell](/wiki/Dick_Powell "Dick Powell")'s reporter Hildy Johnson in the ABC radio drama *The Front Page* (1948).[[22]](#cite_note-22) He was Dave the Dude in the syndicated drama anthology series *[The Damon Runyon Theater](/wiki/Damon_Runyon#Radio "Damon Runyon")* (1948);[[23]](#cite_note-23) Lt. Dundy in the NBC radio series *[The Adventures of Sam Spade](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Sam_Spade "The Adventures of Sam Spade")* (1949–1950);[[24]](#cite_note-24) boss to government special agent [Douglas Fairbanks Jr.](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks_Jr. "Douglas Fairbanks Jr.") in *The Silent Men* (NBC 1951);[[25]](#cite_note-25) and a New Orleans bartender in the NBC adventure drama *Jason and the Golden Fleece* (1952–1953).[[26]](#cite_note-26)
Most prominently, Conrad's deep, resonant voice won him the role of Marshal Matt Dillon on [CBS Radio](/wiki/CBS_Radio "CBS Radio")'s gritty [Western](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)") series *[Gunsmoke](/wiki/Gunsmoke "Gunsmoke")* (1952–1961). The producers originally rejected him for the part because of his ubiquitous presence on so many radio dramas and the familiarity of his voice, but his impressive audition could not be dismissed, and he became the clear choice for the role. Conrad voiced Dillon for the show's nine-year run, and he wrote the June 1953 episode "Sundown".[[27]](#cite_note-27) When *Gunsmoke* was adapted for television in 1955, executives at CBS did not cast Conrad or his radio costars despite a campaign to get them to change their minds.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

His other credits include *[Yours Truly, Johnny Dollar](/wiki/Yours_Truly,_Johnny_Dollar "Yours Truly, Johnny Dollar")*; *[Lux Radio Theatre](/wiki/Lux_Radio_Theatre "Lux Radio Theatre")*; *[Fibber McGee and Molly](/wiki/Fibber_McGee_and_Molly "Fibber McGee and Molly")*; and *[Suspense](/wiki/Suspense_(radio_program) "Suspense (radio program)")*. In "The Wax Works", a 1956 episode of *Suspense*, Conrad performed every part.[[7]](#cite_note-William_Conrad-7) Because of his CBS Radio contract, he sometimes appeared on shows on other networks under the pseudonym "Julius Krelboyne".

In January 1956, Conrad was the announcer on the debut broadcast of *[The CBS Radio Workshop](/wiki/CBS_Radio_Workshop "CBS Radio Workshop")*, a two-part adaptation of [Aldous Huxley](/wiki/Aldous_Huxley "Aldous Huxley")'s *[Brave New World](/wiki/Brave_New_World "Brave New World")*, which Huxley himself narrated. "On the air, *The CBS Radio Workshop* was a lightning rod for ideas," wrote radio historian John Dunning, who cites Conrad's *tour de force* performances in the subsequent broadcasts "The Legend of Jimmy Blue Eyes" (March 23, 1956) and "A Matter of Logic" (June 1, 1956).[[29]](#cite_note-29) Conrad directed and narrated the 1957 episode "Epitaphs", an adaptation of [Edgar Lee Masters](/wiki/Edgar_Lee_Masters "Edgar Lee Masters")'s poetry volume *[Spoon River Anthology](/wiki/Spoon_River_Anthology "Spoon River Anthology")*.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

"And '1489 Words' (Feb. 10, 1957) remains a favorite of many, a powerful Conrad performance proving that one picture is not necessarily worth a thousand words," Dunning concluded. "A lovely way to end a day, a decade, or an era."[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Film")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/The-Killers-1946-McGraw-Conrad.jpg/250px-The-Killers-1946-McGraw-Conrad.jpg)](/wiki/File:The-Killers-1946-McGraw-Conrad.jpg)

[Charles McGraw](/wiki/Charles_McGraw "Charles McGraw") and William Conrad in *[The Killers](/wiki/The_Killers_(1946_film) "The Killers (1946 film)")*, Conrad's film debut

As an actor in feature films, Conrad was often cast as a threatening figure. His most notable role may be the first for which he was credited, as one of the gunmen sent to eliminate [Burt Lancaster](/wiki/Burt_Lancaster "Burt Lancaster") in *[The Killers](/wiki/The_Killers_(1946_film) "The Killers (1946 film)")* (1946). Conrad also appeared in *[Body and Soul](/wiki/Body_and_Soul_(1947_film) "Body and Soul (1947 film)")* (1947), *[Sorry, Wrong Number](/wiki/Sorry,_Wrong_Number "Sorry, Wrong Number")* (1948), *[Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc_(1948_film) "Joan of Arc (1948 film)")* (1948), and *[The Naked Jungle](/wiki/The_Naked_Jungle "The Naked Jungle")* (1954).

In 1961, Conrad moved to the production side of the film business, producing and directing for Warner Bros. film studio.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In 1965 he produced and directed *[Two on a Guillotine](/wiki/Two_on_a_Guillotine "Two on a Guillotine")*, *[My Blood Runs Cold](/wiki/My_Blood_Runs_Cold "My Blood Runs Cold")* and *[Brainstorm](/wiki/Brainstorm_(1965_film) "Brainstorm (1965 film)")* as well as narrating the opening of *[Battle of the Bulge](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Bulge_(1965_film) "Battle of the Bulge (1965 film)")*. *Brainstorm* was a latter-day *film noir* that has come to be regarded as "a minor masterpiece of the 1960s"[[33]](#cite_note-33) and "the final, essential entry in that long line of *films noir* that begins at the end of the Second World War."[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Conrad was the executive producer of *[Countdown](/wiki/Countdown_(1968_film) "Countdown (1968 film)")* (1968), a science-fiction thriller starring [James Caan](/wiki/James_Caan "James Caan") and [Robert Duvall](/wiki/Robert_Duvall "Robert Duvall") that was the major studio feature début of director [Robert Altman](/wiki/Robert_Altman "Robert Altman").

Conrad narrated the documentary *Design for Disaster*, produced by the [Los Angeles City Fire Department](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Fire_Department "Los Angeles City Fire Department"), about the November 1961 [Bel Air](/wiki/Bel_Air,_California "Bel Air, California") [wildfire](/wiki/Bel_Air_Fire "Bel Air Fire") that gutted several neighborhoods, at the time the worst conflagration in Los Angeles history.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

As a token of appreciation from [Jack L. Warner](/wiki/Jack_L._Warner "Jack L. Warner"), head of Warner Bros., Conrad received one of the two original lead-metal falcon statues used in the classic film *[The Maltese Falcon](/wiki/The_Maltese_Falcon_(1941_film) "The Maltese Falcon (1941 film)")* (1941). The falcon sat on a bookshelf in Conrad's house from the 1960s. Standing 11.5 in (29.2 cm) high and weighing 45 lb (20.4 kg), the figurine had been slashed during the making of the film by [Sydney Greenstreet](/wiki/Sydney_Greenstreet "Sydney Greenstreet")'s character Kasper Gutman, leaving deep cuts in its bronze patina. After Conrad's death, the statue was consigned by his widow Tippy Conrad to [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's"), which estimated it would bring $30,000 to $50,000 at auction. In December 1994, Christie's sold the falcon for $398,500. The purchaser was Ronald Winston, president of Harry Winston, Inc. jewelers.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 1996, Winston resold the prop to an unknown European collector "at an enormous profit" – for as much as $1 million.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Late in life, Conrad narrated the opening and closing scenes of the 1991 [Bruce Willis](/wiki/Bruce_Willis "Bruce Willis") feature film *[Hudson Hawk](/wiki/Hudson_Hawk "Hudson Hawk")*.

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Television")]

#### Voice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Voice")]

As "Bill Conrad", he narrated and contributed character voices to [Jay Ward](/wiki/Jay_Ward "Jay Ward")'s animated *[Rocky and Bullwinkle](/wiki/Rocky_and_Bullwinkle "Rocky and Bullwinkle")* series from 1959 to 1964. Conrad's "narrator" voice in this series was far removed from his usual rumbling baritone. He adopted a brisk, high-pitched, nasal tenor that suited the mock urgency of the narratives. He reserved this voice for the *Rocky* cartoons and did not employ it elsewhere.

Conrad narrated *[This Man Dawson](/wiki/This_Man_Dawson "This Man Dawson")*, a 33-episode [syndicated](/wiki/Television_syndication "Television syndication") [crime drama](/wiki/Crime_drama "Crime drama") starring [Keith Andes](/wiki/Keith_Andes "Keith Andes") in the 1959–1960 television season, and then became the familiar voice narrating *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1963_TV_series) "The Fugitive (1963 TV series)")*, starring [David Janssen](/wiki/David_Janssen "David Janssen"), on ABC television from 1963 to 1967. He also introduced Count Basie's Orchestra and Frank Sinatra on Sinatra's 1966 *Live at the Sands* album.

Conrad intoned a rhyming narration heard over the credits of the 1970 [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne") film Western *[Chisum](/wiki/Chisum "Chisum")*. His voice is heard in the [Clio Award](/wiki/Clio_Awards "Clio Awards")-winning 1971 public-service announcement about pollution featuring [Iron Eyes Cody](/wiki/Iron_Eyes_Cody "Iron Eyes Cody"), created for [Earth Day](/wiki/Earth_Day "Earth Day") by [Keep America Beautiful](/wiki/Keep_America_Beautiful "Keep America Beautiful") and the [Ad Council](/wiki/Ad_Council "Ad Council").[[38]](#cite_note-38) From 1973 to 1978, Conrad narrated the TV nature program *[Wild, Wild World of Animals](/wiki/Wild,_Wild_World_of_Animals "Wild, Wild World of Animals")*. Also during the 1970s, he appeared in and narrated a number of episodes for ABC's *American Sportsman* and in the CBS documentary *The Lost Treasure of the Concepcion*. He later narrated *[The Making of Star Wars](/wiki/The_Making_of_Star_Wars "The Making of Star Wars")* (1977), the disaster documentary *Catastrophe!* (1977), the [1978 World Series](/wiki/1978_World_Series "1978 World Series") U.S.-baseball highlight film, *[Buck Rogers in the 25th Century](/wiki/Buck_Rogers_in_the_25th_Century_(TV_series) "Buck Rogers in the 25th Century (TV series)")* (1979), and *[The Rebels](/wiki/The_Rebels_(1979_film) "The Rebels (1979 film)")* (1979). Conrad also performed the role of [Denethor](/wiki/Denethor "Denethor") in the 1980 animated TV version of [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien")'s *[The Return of the King](/wiki/The_Return_of_the_King_(1980_film) "The Return of the King (1980 film)")*. His other voice work included narration for *[The Highwayman](/wiki/The_Highwayman_(TV_series) "The Highwayman (TV series)")* and the "High Flight" sign-off featuring an F-15.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

#### Directing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Directing")]

Conrad directed episodes of [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s *Klondike* in the 1960–1961 season. His other credits as a director include episodes of *[The Rifleman](/wiki/The_Rifleman "The Rifleman")*, *[Bat Masterson](/wiki/Bat_Masterson_(TV_series) "Bat Masterson (TV series)")*, *[Route 66](/wiki/Route_66_(TV_series) "Route 66 (TV series)")*, *[Have Gun – Will Travel](/wiki/Have_Gun_%E2%80%93_Will_Travel "Have Gun – Will Travel")*, *[77 Sunset Strip](/wiki/77_Sunset_Strip "77 Sunset Strip")*, *[Temple Houston](/wiki/Temple_Houston_(TV_series) "Temple Houston (TV series)")* and *[Ripcord](/wiki/Ripcord_(TV_series) "Ripcord (TV series)")*, as well as [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")'s [crime drama](/wiki/Crime_drama "Crime drama") *[Target: The Corruptors!](/wiki/Target:_The_Corruptors! "Target: The Corruptors!")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Acting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Acting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/William_Conrad_Cannon_1972.JPG/250px-William_Conrad_Cannon_1972.JPG)](/wiki/File:William_Conrad_Cannon_1972.JPG)

William Conrad in *[Cannon](/wiki/Cannon_(TV_series) "Cannon (TV series)")* (1972)

Conrad guest-starred in NBC's science-fiction series *The Man and the Challenge* and in the [syndicated](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") skydiving adventure series *Ripcord*, with [Larry Pennell](/wiki/Larry_Pennell "Larry Pennell") and [Ken Curtis](/wiki/Ken_Curtis "Ken Curtis"). In 1962, he starred in an episode of *[The Alfred Hitchcock Hour](/wiki/The_Alfred_Hitchcock_Hour "The Alfred Hitchcock Hour")* and guest-starred in episodes of ABC's [crime drama](/wiki/Crime_drama "Crime drama") *[Target: The Corruptors!](/wiki/Target:_The_Corruptors! "Target: The Corruptors!")*. He appeared as Major Anatole Karzof in a 1984 episode of *Murder, She Wrote* called "Death Takes a Curtain Call".

From 1971 to 1976, he starred in the television detective series *[Cannon](/wiki/Cannon_(TV_series) "Cannon (TV series)")*, which was broadcast on CBS. While starring in the show, he weighed 230 pounds (104 kg), and ballooned to 260 pounds (118 kg) or more.

"I heard that [Weight Watchers](/wiki/WW_International "WW International") had banned its members from watching the show, but it turned out to be a gag," Conrad said in 1973. "The publicist for Weight Watchers did call and suggest that I have lunch with their president. I said sure – if I could pick the restaurant."[[41]](#cite_note-41)

From the early 1980s to the early 1990s, he starred in two other TV series, *[Nero Wolfe](/wiki/Nero_Wolfe_(1981_TV_series) "Nero Wolfe (1981 TV series)")* (in [the title role](/wiki/Nero_Wolfe "Nero Wolfe") of [Rex Stout](/wiki/Rex_Stout "Rex Stout")'s brilliant detective, 1981) with [Lee Horsley](/wiki/Lee_Horsley "Lee Horsley"), and *[Jake and the Fatman](/wiki/Jake_and_the_Fatman "Jake and the Fatman")* (1987–1992) with [Joe Penny](/wiki/Joe_Penny "Joe Penny").

#### Hosting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Hosting")]

Throughout the 1970s and into the 1980s, Conrad served as the armchair-and-fireside host of the *CBS All-American Thanksgiving Day Parade* morning broadcasts in which he anchored the network's annual holiday telecast of parades from around the U.S. and Canada, including parades from Detroit, Hawaii, New York City, Philadelphia, and Toronto.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

#### Other appearances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Other appearances")]

In the 1970s and 1980s, Conrad appeared in a few [Pittway](/wiki/Pittway "Pittway") [First Alert](/wiki/First_Alert "First Alert") [Smoke Alarm](/wiki/Smoke_detector "Smoke detector") commercials as the host, explaining the need for the alarms.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Personal life")]

In 1957, Conrad was married to former fashion model Susan Randall (1928–1979), and the couple had one son, Christopher.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In 1980, Conrad married Tipton "Tippy" Stringer (1930–2010), a broadcast pioneer and the widow of NBC newscaster [Chet Huntley](/wiki/Chet_Huntley "Chet Huntley").[[47]](#cite_note-47) She helped manage his career during their 14-year marriage.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

## Hobbies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hobbies")]

Conrad was an avid outdoorsman and accomplished fisherman. Having been known for his prowess using light tackle, as documented in the magazine *[Field & Stream](/wiki/Field_%26_Stream "Field & Stream")*, on May 23, 1972, in the Yucatán Channel of Mexico, Conrad caught a 62 lb, 4 oz sailfish on thread-like 6-lb-test line.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Death")]

Conrad died of a heart attack at age 73 in Los Angeles on February 11, 1994.[[50]](#cite_note-50) He was buried in the Lincoln Terrace section of [Forest Lawn, Hollywood Hills Cemetery](/wiki/Forest_Lawn_Memorial_Park_(Hollywood_Hills) "Forest Lawn Memorial Park (Hollywood Hills)"), California.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Conrad&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Recognition")]

Conrad was posthumously elected to the [National Radio Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Radio_Hall_of_Fame "National Radio Hall of Fame") in 1997, and also to the Broadcasting and Cable Hall of Fame.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)
